title: Blood on Wool
stainChemistry: Blood is hemoglobin — an iron-carrying protein with a heme chromophore that gives it red color. On wool (also a protein fiber, made of keratin), hemoglobin forms hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions with the keratin chains. Fresh blood contains Fe²⁺ (ferrous iron, soluble). As blood ages or is exposed to heat, the iron oxidizes to Fe³⁺ (ferric iron, insoluble brown). The oxidized iron cross-links with wool's peptide backbone, creating a bond that becomes increasingly difficult to break. Wool's scale structure adds complexity — moisture, heat, and agitation cause those scales to lock together (felting), which is irreversible.
whyThisWorks: Cold water keeps the hemoglobin soluble and prevents thermal cross-linking with keratin. NSD surrounds and lifts the protein without attacking the wool fiber. Reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) donates electrons to break the iron-heme chromophore — converting visible Fe³⁺ back to soluble, colorless form. The entire approach avoids enzymes (which would digest wool's own keratin), avoids heat (which accelerates both stain-setting and felting), and uses acetic acid to restore wool's natural pH of ~5 after treatment.

spottingProtocol.equipment: White cotton pad or folded towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Start dry. Press a clean white cotton pad against the stain — don't rub. You're absorbing any liquid blood that hasn't bonded yet. If the blood is still wet, you'll see red transfer to the pad. Keep blotting with fresh pads until transfer slows. This prevents you from pushing dissolved hemoglobin deeper into the wool's scale structure during the wet phase. Never scrub — friction plus moisture on wool equals felting.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Spray bottle with cold distilled water, white towel underneath
spottingProtocol.instruction: Spray cold distilled water onto the BACK of the stain. You're pushing the hemoglobin OUT, not deeper in. Place a white towel underneath to catch runoff. You'll see pale red in the rinse water — that's loose hemoglobin flushing out. Blot between each flush with a clean pad. Three flushes, 40 seconds each. Cold water is non-negotiable — anything above 30°C starts denaturing hemoglobin AND risks felting the wool. This step alone removes 30-40% of fresh blood.

spottingProtocol.agent: Apply NSD (neutral synthetic detergent) — 1 tsp per cup cold water
spottingProtocol.technique: NSD surround and lift — back side
spottingProtocol.equipment: Spray bottle or eyedropper, white cotton pads, pressing cloths
spottingProtocol.instruction: Spray diluted NSD (1 teaspoon per cup cold distilled water) onto the back of the stain. NSD encapsulates the hemoglobin molecules without attacking wool's keratin — it surrounds and lifts, doesn't dissolve. You'll see the remaining red start to fade as the detergent breaks the hydrophobic grip between hemoglobin and keratin. Let it dwell 3-4 minutes. Blot with a clean white pad — you should see color transferring to the pad. Repeat 2-3 times until fresh pad shows minimal color. Do not agitate or rub.

Flush all NSD residue before the next agent. Spray cold water on the back, blot immediately. You'll feel the texture shift from slick (soapy) to smooth (clean wool). Two flushes minimum. Residual detergent left behind can trap dissolved hemoglobin in the fiber and cause a shadow stain when it dries. The wool should feel like wool again — slightly rough from the scale structure, not slippery.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Eyedropper or spray, white towel, rubber gloves, ventilation
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush thoroughly with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to remove all chemistry residue. Work from back of fabric. Blot — do not rub. All oxidizer and enzyme residue must be fully removed before drying.

spottingProtocol.agent: Flush + neutralize with acetic acid (28% GAA diluted 1:10, or 28% Acetic Acid (diluted))
spottingProtocol.instruction: Wool's natural pH is around 5. The reducing H₂O₂ (3%) for white/light fabrics only. Spray diluted acetic acid (1:10 GAA, or straight 5% 28% Acetic Acid (diluted)) onto the treated area. This closes the wool's scale structure back to normal and prevents residual chemistry from continuing to work on the fiber. You won't see dramatic change — that's correct. You're balancing pH, not removing stain. After 2 minutes, flush one final time with cold distilled water and blot dry.
spottingProtocol.suggestedProducts: GAA (28% glacial acetic acid), 28% Acetic Acid (diluted)

spottingProtocol.agent: Air dry flat — no heat, no hanging
spottingProtocol.equipment: Clean white towel, flat drying surface
spottingProtocol.instruction: Lay the garment flat on a clean white towel. Reshape gently — wool is malleable when wet. Do NOT hang (gravity plus moisture stretches wool) and do NOT use any heat source (dryer, iron, heat gun, direct sun). The weight of water in wet wool combined with hanging causes distortion that's difficult to reverse. As the wool dries, you'll see the scale structure settle and the natural texture return. Check the stain once fully dry — fresh blood treated promptly should be completely gone. If a faint shadow remains, it's likely oxidized iron that resisted the reducing H₂O₂ (3%) for white/light fabrics only.

homeSolutions: FRESH BLOOD (within 30 minutes): Cold water flush immediately — hold the stain under cold running water for 60 seconds. Mix 1 teaspoon mild NSD (pH-neutral, like baby shampoo) in 1 cup cold water. Soak for 15 minutes, then blot. Repeat. This alone handles most fresh blood on wool.

OLDER BLOOD (dry but <24 hours): Cold water soak for 30 minutes. Then mix 1 tablespoon 28% Acetic Acid (diluted) in 1 cup cold water, soak another 15 minutes. The acidity helps loosen the hemoglobin bonds. Blot gently, repeat if needed. Air dry flat.

SET BLOOD (24+ hours, turned brown): The iron has oxidized. Try H₂O₂ 3% diluted 1:1 with cold water — TEST on hidden area first, as it can lighten dyed wool. Apply to stain, blot after 2 minutes. If the stain persists or the garment is valuable, this is professional territory.
materialWarnings: NEVER use hot water — heat denatures hemoglobin AND causes irreversible felting of wool fibers. The combination is catastrophic: the stain sets permanently while the fabric shrinks and matts.

NEVER use enzyme detergents (OxiClean, Biz, any 'bio' laundry product) — these contain protein-digesting enzymes that will attack wool's keratin structure, weakening and eventually dissolving the fiber.
NEVER use chlorine bleach — dissolves wool's disulfide bonds, turning the fiber yellow and then destroying it entirely.
NEVER use strong alkaline solutions (ammonia >2%, sodium hydroxide) — alkalinity opens wool's scale structure and accelerates both stain penetration and felting.
NEVER rub or agitate vigorously — friction plus moisture on wool causes mechanical felting. Press and blot only.
ALWAYS use distilled water — tap water minerals can interact with blood chemistry and spotting agents, leaving white mineral deposits in the treated area.
escalation: If the stain persists after two rounds of reducing bleach: the iron has likely fully oxidized and cross-linked with the keratin. A professional dry cleaner with access to PERC or hydrocarbon solvents can sometimes lift what water-based spotting cannot. Tell them: 'Blood stain, wool fiber, I've already tried NSD and reducing bleach — the iron appears to have oxidized.' They'll understand. If the garment is valuable (cashmere blend, designer piece), skip home treatment entirely and go straight to a professional cleaner who specializes in delicate fibers. For very old brown blood stains (weeks+), a chelating agent like sodium EDTA may help sequester the remaining iron, but this is advanced professional chemistry.

household.name: Woolite Delicates
household.note: pH-NSD safe for wool. Your NSD substitute for home use.

household.name: 28% Acetic Acid (diluted) (5% acetic acid)
household.note: pH neutralizer and mild protein stain lifter. Safe on wool.

household.name: H₂O₂ 3%
household.note: Mild oxidizer for older stains. TEST first on hidden area — can lighten dyed wool.

professional.name: Mulsolite® (NSD)
professional.note: Industry-standard NSD. Safe on all fibers including wool.

professional.name: StreePene® (reducing bleach)
professional.note: Professional reducing bleach for iron-based stains. Safe on wool with timed application (≤60 sec).

professional.name: GAA (28% Glacial Acetic Acid)
professional.note: Professional-grade pH neutralizer. Restores wool's natural acid balance after treatment.

customerExplanation: Blood is a protein stain that bonds directly to wool because wool is also a protein fiber — they're chemically attracted to each other. The red color comes from iron in the blood. We use cold water and a gentle detergent to lift the protein, then a mild reducing agent to break the iron's color bond. The key with wool is staying cold and gentle — hot water or rough handling would lock the stain in permanently and damage the fabric's texture.
commonMistakes: Using hot water — the most common panic response. Hot water denatures the hemoglobin (sets the stain permanently) AND starts felting the wool. By the time someone realizes the mistake, both the stain and the fabric damage are irreversible.

Using enzyme-based cleaners — products like OxiClean or 'bio' laundry detergents contain Protein enzymes that digest protein. Wool IS protein. The enzyme can't tell the difference between blood protein and wool keratin. The stain might fade, but the fiber weakens.

Rubbing or scrubbing — wool's scale structure locks under friction when wet. People scrub blood stains thinking more pressure = more removal. Instead, the stain spreads AND the area starts to felt. Press and blot, never scrub.

Skipping the acetic acid flush — residual alkalinity from spotting chemistry opens wool's scale structure. People rinse with water and call it done, but the pH imbalance slowly damages the fiber over days. The acid flush takes 2 minutes and protects the garment long-term.
scienceNote: Hemoglobin's iron atom sits inside a porphyrin ring (the heme group) that absorbs green light and reflects red — that's why blood is red. When blood hits wool, the protein chains in hemoglobin form hydrogen bonds with keratin's peptide backbone, while the hydrophobic heme group nestles into wool's lipid layer (lanolin residue). Fresh blood (Fe²⁺) is relatively easy to flush because the iron is in its reduced, soluble state. But oxidation converts Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺, which is insoluble and forms coordination bonds with keratin's nitrogen atoms — essentially rusting into the fiber. Reducing bleach reverses this by donating electrons back to the iron, converting it from insoluble Fe³⁺ to soluble Fe²⁺. It's the same chemistry that makes rust removers work on metal — just adapted for textile use.
callPro: If the blood stain is more than a week old, has turned brown/black, or the garment is high-value: go straight to a professional dry cleaner who handles delicates. Aged blood on wool is one of the harder professional challenges because the iron cross-links with keratin over time. Tell them the stain's age and whether any home treatment was attempted. If the wool feels matted or stiff after your attempt, the fiber may have felted — a professional textile restoration specialist is the only option, and even they may not be able to fully reverse felting damage.
difficulty_factors: Protein-on-protein fiber — hemoglobin bonds directly to keratin through shared chemistry
Iron oxidation — Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺ conversion makes aged stains dramatically harder
Wool felting risk — heat, agitation, or alkalinity at any step can permanently damage the fiber
No enzyme option — Protein (the standard protein stain tool) is off-limits because it digests wool
Reducing bleach timing — sodium hydrosulfite is effective but must be removed within 60 seconds
Temperature management — cold water throughout, no exceptions
solventNote: This protocol uses wet-side spotting only — safe for all solvent systems. For PERC/HC shops: dryclean normally after treatment. For wet clean shops: gentle wet clean cycle with wool program after spotting.
